title: The Glass Menagerie by Tennessee Williams | Character Sketch in Hindi | BA Eng Hons
description:
Few plays trap their characters quite like Tennessee Williams does in The Glass Menagerie — Tom suffocates under obligation, Amanda drowns in illusion, and fragile Laura retreats into a world of glass figurines because reality has already broken her once. Written as a "memory play," this semi-autobiographical masterpiece explores escapism, family dysfunction, and the crushing weight of unfulfilled dreams — themes that make every character sketch in The Glass Menagerie feel less like an academic exercise and more like an emotional reckoning. If you're preparing for BA English Honours exams at DU, SOL, or IGNOU, understanding these characters is the difference between passing and truly understanding the text.

Amanda’s son and Laura’s brother, Tom play a dual role in the play as both the narrator and protagonist. The play is from the perspective of Tom’s memories.
Amanda Wingfield
Tom and Laura’s mother. Amanda was a Southern belle in her youth, and she clings to this romantic vision of her past rather than accepting her current circumstances of poverty and abandonment.
Laura Wingfield
Tom’s sister and Amanda’s daughter. Laura is deeply fragile, both emotionally and physically: she is painfully shy, and a childhood illness has left one leg slightly shorter than the other.
Jim O’Connor
The Gentleman Caller whose arrival in scene six spurs the play’s climax. Tennessee Williams’s stage directions describe Jim as “a nice, ordinary, young man.” Jim works with Tom at the warehouse.
Mr. Wingfield
The absent father of Tom and Laura and husband of Amanda. He never appears on stage, but his portrait dominates the living room, and his presence looms throughout the play.
